Contents of /Users/krestnikov/.claude/projects/-Users-krestnikov-giga-compression-drives-truth/memory/MEMORY.md (user's auto-memory, persists across conversations):

# Compression Truth Bias — Project Memory

## Publication
- [project_neurips_submission.md](project_neurips_submission.md) — NeurIPS 2026, Sydney, Dec 6-12. Abstract May 4, paper May 6, notification Sep 24
- [reference_fenogenova_review.md](reference_fenogenova_review.md) — Alena Fenogenova's review of v2, basis for v3 revisions

## Project Status
- Experiment 1 (math, tiny models): COMPLETE. 35 models trained.
- Experiment 2 (observations): COMPLETE. 20 models. Weak effect confirmed.
- Experiment 3 (conditions C/D/E): COMPLETE. 12 models trained.
- Experiment 4 (scaling): COMPLETE. Small (11M) 4 seeds, medium (26M) 4 seeds, large (86M) 4 random + 1 coherent.
- Experiment F (multi-rule): COMPLETE. 16 models (N=2,3,5,10 × 4 seeds).
- Experiment G (synthetic world, natural language): COMPLETE. 8 models (random + coherent × 4 seeds).
- Experiment: World multi-alt (N=2,4,8,16): COMPLETE. 16 models. Gradual rise 40%→60%, no phase transition.
- Experiment: World contradictory: COMPLETE. 4 models. acc≈47.5%, no truth bias.
- Paper draft: `paper_draft_ru.md` (Russian), `paper_draft_en.md` (English)
- Experiment I (chained tasks): COMPLETE. 4 tiny + 4 small + 2 large = 10 models.
  - Tiny: 70.9%±1.2%, Small: 64.2%±1.5%, Large: 60.6%±1.2%
  - INVERSE SCALING: accuracy DROPS with model size (opposite of random errors)
  - KEY RESULT: compressor power is double-edged sword
- **STATUS: PUBLISHED on arXiv — https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11749. 160+ models. All experiments complete.**
- Experiment K (Wikipedia entity substitution): COMPLETE. 32 models (4 sizes × 2 modes × 4 seeds). MLX on M4 Pro.
  - Random: tiny 69.6%±0.1%, small 70.7%±0.4%, medium 71.5%±0.8%, large 71.4%±0.7%
  - Coherent: tiny 48.7%±0.5%, small 46.6%±0.4%, medium 46.4%±0.8%, large 45.9%±1.5%
  - KEY: truth bias confirmed on REAL Wikipedia text with NER entity substitution
  - Weaker than math (71% vs 89%) but highly significant across all 16 random seeds
  - Coherent errors: model slightly PREFERS them (<50%), consistent with math results
  - Per-type: GPE/LOC/NORP best (77-82%), ORG/PERSON moderate (65-70%), CARDINAL weakest (61%)
- Experiment J (denoising): COMPLETE. 48 models (14 tiny + 14 small + 14 medium + 10 large). PyTorch on Modal.com T4 GPU.
  - J1 (1:1 random): tiny 65.3% → small 74.6% → medium 81.1% → large 85.2%
  - J2 (1:1 coherent): tiny 43.5% → small 44.5% → medium 45.8% → large 51.0% (at chance)
  - J3 (1:2 random): tiny 59.0% → small 68.6% → medium 73.6% → large 75.2%
  - J4 (1:4 random): tiny 56.6% → small 64.9% → medium 66.6% → large 65.8% (plateau)
  - J5 (no correct): tiny 53.8% → small 59.9% → medium 58.1% → large 56.1%
  - KEY: signal extraction scales with model size; coherent errors remain at chance; J4 plateaus
  - Standard vs denoising J1: 83→65% (tiny), 88→75% (small), 81% (medium), 89→85% (large) — gap closes with scale

## Key Results
- Random errors 50/50: ΔLoss = +0.0115 (4/4 seeds)
- Coherent errors 50/50: ΔLoss = −0.0004 (0/4 seeds) — NO truth bias
- Contradictory errors 50/50: ΔLoss = +0.0005 (4/4 seeds) — weak bias
- Breakpoint at ~15% correct data (10/90 inverts)
- **Phase 2 (observations):** ΔLoss = +0.0002 to +0.0008 (0-50% obs). WARNING confirmed: discrepancies are regular/compressible. 100% obs breaks training (loss ~0.32).
- **Paired eval (random 50/50):** ΔLoss = +0.048, accuracy = 83%, p<1e-6. Effect 4× stronger than corpus-level.
- **Paired eval (random proportions):** 40/60→79%, 30/70→75%, 20/80→69%, all p<1e-6
- **Paired eval (coherent 50/50):** ΔLoss = −0.002, accuracy = 47%, p≈1.0. No truth bias.
- **Paired eval (contradictory 50/50):** ΔLoss ≈ 0, accuracy = 49%. At noise level.
- **Paired eval (C/D/E):** accuracy ≈ 49% — NO truth bias. Corpus-level ΔLoss was artifact of text statistics.
- KEY INSIGHT: Corpus-level eval for C/D/E was confounded. Only random errors produce real truth bias.
- Per-type accuracy (random paired): algebra 99.9% > arithmetic 94% > derivative 72% > equation 65%
- **Paired eval 10/90:** ΔLoss = +0.017, accuracy = 67%, p<1e-88. Truth bias PERSISTS even at 10/90 in paired eval (corpus-level inverts).
- **Scaling (tiny→small→medium):** Paired accuracy 83.6%→88.4%→88.5%, ΔLoss +0.048→+0.063→+0.067. Truth bias INCREASES with model size but accuracy plateaus at medium.
- **Scaling coherent (small):** accuracy=49.6%, no truth bias — same as tiny. Coherent errors remain indistinguishable at any size.
- **Scaling large (86M):** 4 seeds: 88.3%, 89.4%, 88.7%, 90.0%. Mean=89.1%±0.6%, ΔLoss=+0.070. Coherent large: acc=51.8%, no bias.
- **Scaling trend:** tiny 83.1% → small 88.4% → medium 88.4% → large 89.1%. Truth bias increases with size.
- **Multi-rule (Exp F):** N=2→87.4%, N=3→89.4%, N=5→89.9%, N=10→91.5%, all p<1e-6.
  - Accuracy HIGHER than random (83.6%) for all N≥2 — multi-rule errors less compressible than purely random.
  - Monotonically increases with N. Phase transition at N=1→2 (49%→87%).
- **Synthetic world (Exp G, natural language):**
  - Random errors 50/50: acc=57.7%±1.7%, ΔLoss=+0.034 (all 4 seeds p<0.001). Effect WEAKER than math (83→58%) but significant.
  - Coherent errors 50/50: acc=46.6%±1.7% (below chance). No truth bias — same pattern as math.
  - Per-type: minerals best (65-71%), plants (56-68%), animals near chance, potions near chance.
  - Key: truth bias TRANSFERS to natural language but is weaker. Coherent errors still undetectable.
- **Coherent proportions paired eval:**
  - 50/50→47.2%, 40/60→27.8%, 30/70→14.7%, 20/80→9.6%
  - Model actively prefers coherent errors when they're majority (91% at 20/80!)
  - vs random: 50/50→83%, 40/60→79%, 30/70→75%, 20/80→69%
  - Key: truth has NO privilege when errors are equally compressible — pure frequency wins
- **Cross-domain falsification (Exp H):** COMPLETE. 16 models.
  - Derivative accuracy: 0%→35.2%, 10%→39.4%, 25%→56.0%, 50%→45.4%
  - Other types unchanged (~48-51%). Cross-domain tasks ADDRESS only derivative coherence.
  - Effect non-monotonic: peak at 25%, drops at 50% (corpus dilution)
  - Key: first evidence that cross-domain data can break coherent errors SELECTIVELY
  - Files: `data/generate_math_crossdomain.py`, `run_crossdomain.sh`

## Experiment Design (5 conditions for false theory)
- A: No observations (baseline, done) → ΔLoss = +0.0005 (4/4 seeds, weak positive)
- B: Bare discrepancies (done, Exp 2) → ΔLoss weak (+0.0005-0.0008) ✓
  - Discrepancies ARE regular/compressible as warned
- C: Ad hoc escape hatches → ΔLoss = +0.0025 (4/4) ✓
- D: Systematic correction → ΔLoss = +0.0026 (4/4) — SURPRISE: D ≈ C, not D ≈ 0
- E: Vague predictions → ΔLoss = +0.0015 (4/4) ✓
- Actual order: D ≈ C > E > B > A (expected C > B > E > D ≈ A)
- Key insight: correction step itself is the information cost, not just ad-hoc-ness
- Corpora for C/D/E already generated at `data/corpus/train_cond{C,D,E}_50_50.txt`
- Test sets: `test_correct_dual.txt`, `test_incorrect_dual.txt`

## Exp F Results (multi-rule / conspiratorial errors) — COMPLETE
- N rules per task type, randomly assigned per problem
- Results: N=2→87.4%, N=3→89.4%, N=5→89.9%, N=10→91.5%
- Surprise: all N≥2 ABOVE random (83.6%), not between coherent and random
- Phase transition N=1→2 is the key finding: one rule = compressible, two = not
- Files: `data/generate_math_multirule.py`, `run_multirule.sh`

## Theoretical Framework
Compression pressure = computational analogue of Popper's falsifiability.
- Type 1: Falsifiable true → predictions match → max compression
- Type 2: Falsifiable false → discrepancies memorized → poor compression
- Type 3a: Unfalsifiable (vague) → can't predict → intermediate
- Type 3b: Unfalsifiable (ad hoc escape hatches) → system grows → anti-compression

## NeurIPS Revision (2026-03-14)
- **П.1 (compression measure): DONE** — gzip/bz2 on completion-only segments, 9 conditions
  - Spearman ρ=0.68, p=0.042 between compression delta and paired accuracy
  - Files: `analysis/compression_measure.py`, `analysis/plot_compression_vs_accuracy.py`
  - Results: `results/compression_ratios.json`, `results/figure_compression_vs_accuracy.png`
  - Added to paper: Section 3.5 (Table C1, Figure C2)
- **П.2 (learning curves): DONE** — paired accuracy at intermediate checkpoints
  - All sizes reach behavioral plateau by step 3000-4000
  - Large model NOT undertrained: 88.8% at step 3000, stable through 5000
  - Files: `analysis/plot_learning_curves.py`, `results/learning_curves.json`, `results/figure_learning_curves.png`
  - Added to paper: Section 7.2 (Table LC1, Figure LC)
- **П.5 (convergence-matched): NOT NEEDED** — learning curves show plateau
- **П.6 (generation sanity check): DONE** — greedy decode + SymPy, 500 tasks × 8 models
  - Random-trained: 30.5%±1.7%, Coherent-trained: 20.8%±3.6%, paired t-test p=0.013
  - Arithmetic = 0% for both (too hard for tiny model generation)
  - Files: `training/eval_generation.py`, `run_generation_check.sh`, `run_generation_check_full.sh`
  - Added to paper: Section 8.4 (Table G1)
- **П.7 (NLL histogram): DONE** — per-pair NLL(inc)-NLL(cor) distribution
  - Random: right-skewed, mean=0.048 >> median=0.025, heavy tail
  - Coherent: symmetric around 0
  - Explains metric divergence (mean DLoss vs accuracy)
  - Files: `analysis/plot_nll_histogram.py`, `results/nll_pair_deltas.json`, `results/figure_nll_histogram.png`
  - Added to paper: Section 8.1 (Figure H1)
- **П.3 (BPE tokenization): DONE** — sentencepiece BPE vocab=1000, 8 models on MLX (no GPU needed!)
  - Random BPE: 85.6%±0.2% (vs char 83.1%±2.0%) — effect SURVIVES and STRENGTHENS
  - Coherent BPE: 45.9%±1.4% (vs char 47.2%±2.7%) — still no truth bias
  - Files: `training/tokenizer.py` (BPETokenizer class), `run_bpe_experiment.sh`, `results/bpe_*`
  - Added to paper: Section 8.4 (Table BPE1)
- **П.4 (text restructuring): DONE** — abstract 180 words, Discussion 3 themes, Conclusion 1 paragraph
- **ALL THREE VULNERABILITIES CLOSED. All mandatory experiments complete.**
- **Remaining:** П.8-9 (polish), П.11 (LaTeX)

## Current Phase: REWRITE around denoising framing (2026-03-15)
- **Decision:** rebuild paper with denoising as primary setup, arXiv will be updated
- NeurIPS NOT yet submitted, no constraints on restructuring
- Denoising framing: "model sees contradictory answers to same problem — what does it prefer?"
- Need to rerun: scaling (medium/large), multi-rule, BPE, synthetic world — all in denoising setup
- Original experiments → Supporting Evidence / Appendix
- Previously: PUBLISHED on arXiv (https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11749)
- P0: Results integrity — **DONE**
  - `scripts/collect_results.py` → `results_master.csv` + `scripts/tables.md`
  - ΔLoss convention fixed (Tabl 3 sign error, Cond A was −0.0003→+0.0005)
  - Binomial test (16/16 seeds, p=3.05e-5) + bootstrap CI in all tables
  - `docs/refs_audit.md` — fixed DeMoss (fabricated), Pan (wrong authors), Ravfogel (wrong co-authors)
  - Contradictory eval run (4 seeds had no eval files)
- P1: Reframe thesis — **DONE**
  - New title: "Когда сжатие предпочитает истину?"
  - Abstract/intro rewritten with 3 caveats (text not world, frequency, description length)
  - Popper → Discussion only (section 7.2, interpretive analogy with limits)
  - Neutral condition names in experiments (no гомеопатия/астрология)
  - Contribution statement in Related Work
  - Missing refs added (Shannon, Solomonoff, Hutter)
- P2: Paired evaluation — **DONE** (28 models evaluated, 2026-03-06)
  - All paired evals complete: random (4 proportions), coherent, contradictory, C/D/E
  - C/D/E corpus-level ΔLoss was artifact — paired eval shows no truth bias
  - [ ] Length control, full-dataset eval, factor analysis
- P3: New experiments — scaling DONE, multi-rule DONE, synthetic world DONE
  - Figures 6+7 created: `analysis/plot_scaling_multirule.py` → `results/figure6_scaling.png`, `results/figure7_multirule.png`
  - Paper updated with large scaling + multi-rule sections
- P4: Novelty positioning, English translation, submission
- Key decision: title changed to "Compression Favors Consistency, Not Truth" / "Сжатие предпочитает согласованность, а не истину"
- Named principle: **Compression–Consistency Principle** — gradient descent favors the most compressible hypothesis, not truth. Truth bias is a side effect of error incoherence.
- Conceptual figure added to Section 3.5: `results/figure_conceptual.png`, script `analysis/plot_conceptual.py`
- Intro rewritten: problem → existing explanations → our hypothesis → what we show

## User Preferences
- User wants historical examples alongside modern pseudoscience (phlogiston, miasma, geocentrism)
- Paper in Russian
- Don't add intermediate proportions (45/55, 65/35, etc.) — too much

## Infrastructure
- PyTorch port: `training_torch/` (model.py, train.py, eval_paired.py)
- MLX original: `training/` (unchanged)
- Modal deployment: `modal_run.py`
- Results stored in Modal volume "compression-truth-results" and locally in `results/experiment_j_denoising.json`

1	# Error Structure Determines Correctness Preference in Contradictory Training Data
2	
3	## Abstract
4	
5	Language models trained on contradictory data often prefer correct answers, yet the mechanism behind this preference is poorly understood. Without such understanding, we cannot predict when this implicit filtering will fail -- a question critical for controlling model behavior on noisy real-world corpora. We hypothesize that next-token prediction, as a compression process, favors whichever answer cluster has lower description length; truth benefits only when errors lack internal structure. We train transformers (3.5M--1B parameters) from scratch on controlled corpora where the same problem appears with both correct and incorrect solutions, systematically varying the structure of errors. We demonstrate three findings: (a) when errors are random, models develop a correctness preference scaling from 65% to 85% with model size; (b) when errors follow a single coherent alternative rule, this preference vanishes entirely (~45--51%); (c) the transition is sharp -- two competing wrong rules suffice to restore correctness preference (47% -> 78%). The pattern reproduces on real Wikipedia text (71% vs 46%), across architectures (GPT-2 and Qwen3), and at 1B scale on a mixed real-text corpus (77% vs 47%). These results support the hypothesis that, in controlled contradictory corpora, model preference is driven by the relative compressibility of competing answer systems rather than by truth per se.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Introduction
10	
11	Real-world training corpora are noisy: the same question may receive contradictory answers across different documents. Yet language models trained on such data tend to prefer correct information -- and this implicit filtering is increasingly relied upon in high-stakes applications from medical QA to legal reasoning. If we do not understand *why* this filtering works, we cannot predict *when* it will fail. This is not merely a theoretical concern: coordinated misinformation campaigns, systematic biases in training data, and internally consistent pseudoscientific frameworks all present cases where errors may share enough structure to evade whatever mechanism produces truthful preferences.
12	
13	Several explanations have been offered. Scaling improves factual performance (Kadavath et al., 2022). RLHF steers outputs toward human preferences. Frequency matters: factual accuracy correlates with how often correct information appears in training data (Elazar et al., 2022; Joshi et al., 2024; Kandpal et al., 2023). Recent work has found truth-correlated internal representations (Burns et al., 2023; Marks & Tegmark, 2023; Burger et al., 2024) and established that calibrated models must hallucinate at a rate bounded by the corpus's monofact rate (Kalai & Vempala, 2024). Yet none of these explain why the training objective would favor one answer over another when both appear at equal frequency in the same format.
14	
15	We propose that the answer lies in the structure of errors. Minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing code length (Shannon, 1948; Deletang et al., 2024), linking LLM training to the Minimum Description Length principle (Rissanen, 1978; Grunwald, 2007). A correct rule system compresses into a compact representation; diverse errors must be memorized individually. But a *coherent* false system -- internally consistent, just wrong -- compresses equally well, and the preference vanishes.
16	
17	We test this hypothesis in a controlled experimental setting. We train transformers from 3.5M to 1B parameters from scratch on corpora where each mathematical problem appears with both correct and incorrect solutions, systematically varying the structure of errors. This design isolates error structure as the independent variable while holding frequency, format, and domain constant.
18	
19	Our contribution is a single experimentally validated hypothesis: **in controlled contradictory corpora, the compression objective favors consistency, not truth.** We show that (a) random errors produce a correctness preference scaling from 65% to 85%; (b) a single coherent alternative rule eliminates this preference entirely; (c) two competing rules restore it, pinpointing the compressibility boundary; (d) the same contrast reproduces on real Wikipedia text, across architectures, and at 1B scale on a mixed real-text corpus.
20	
21	## 2. Related Work
22	
23	**Prediction as compression.** The link between prediction and compression traces to Shannon (1948) and was formalized by Solomonoff (1964) and Rissanen's MDL principle (1978; Grunwald, 2007). Deletang et al. (2024) showed that LLMs are universal compressors. Huang et al. (2024) discovered a linear correlation (r ~ -0.95) between compression quality and benchmark performance. Wan & Mei (2025) proved that LLM training approximates Solomonoff induction. Pan et al. (2025) linked compression to knowledge acquisition and scaling; Chlon et al. (2025) linked compression failures to hallucination. We build on the MDL framework by experimentally varying the description length of false answer systems.
24	
25	**Internal representations and world models.** Compression can give rise to structured internal representations: Li et al. (2023a) found board representations in an Othello model, Gurnee & Tegmark (2024) discovered linear space-time representations in Llama-2, and several studies found truth-correlated representations (Marks & Tegmark, 2023; Burns et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023b; Azaria & Mitchell, 2023; Ravfogel et al., 2025). Our work operates at the behavioral level: we identify data-level conditions under which compression produces a preference for correct completions, leaving activation-level analysis for future work.
26	
27	**Truthfulness and data statistics.** Joshi et al. (2024) linked truthfulness to "personas" in pretraining data. Elazar et al. (2022) demonstrated frequency dependence. Kandpal et al. (2023) showed a direct relationship between document count and accuracy. Li et al. (2024) studied LLM preferences under conflicting knowledge and found that formality and surface quality predict which version the model favors -- a consistency-driven explanation complementary to ours. Unlike these analyses, we fix frequency, format, and surface quality, varying only the compressibility of the error system itself.
28	
29	**Simplicity bias and noisy labels.** Neural networks prefer simple functions (Valle-Perez et al., 2019; Mingard et al., 2021; Goldblum et al., 2024). The noisy labels literature directly parallels our setup: Zhang et al. (2017) showed memorization of random labels; Rolnick et al. (2017) showed robustness to massive label noise. Grokking connects to compression as a memorization-to-generalization transition (Nanda et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). Our experiments extend this line by showing that "structured noise" -- coherent errors -- is not filtered out. To our knowledge, systematic variation of error compressibility in a denoising setting has not been studied before.
30	
31	## 3. Methodology
32	
33	**Overview.** We test the hypothesis that next-token prediction favors whichever answer system has lower description length, regardless of its truth value. To isolate this, we train models from scratch on controlled corpora where each problem appears with contradictory answers, varying only the structure of errors. The core experiments compare random errors (high description length) against coherent errors (low description length) at equal frequency (Section 4.1), then probe the boundary with multi-rule errors of intermediate compressibility (Section 4.2). Transfer to natural language is tested via Wikipedia entity substitution (Section 4.3).
34	
35	### 3.1 Hypothesis and MDL Framing
36	
37	Consider a corpus where each problem appears with a correct answer (theory $T_1$) and an alternative ($T_2$). An MDL learner (Rissanen, 1978; Grunwald, 2007) minimizes $L(M) + L(D|M)$. When $K(T_2) \gg K(T_1)$ (random errors), the false system's description length grows with corpus size, favoring $T_1$. When $K(T_2) \approx K(T_1)$ (coherent errors), both are compact and the learner has no basis to prefer one over the other. With N competing false rules, a random "selector" (which rule applies to which problem) adds $\log N$ bits per problem, progressively degrading the false cluster's compressibility.
38	
39	Throughout, "truth" means correctness of mathematical derivations and factual accuracy of Wikipedia paragraphs -- models compress text, not reality. The compressibility gap is domain-dependent and smaller in natural language than in formal math (Section 4.3). Additional limitations are discussed in Section 6.
40	
41	### 3.2 Models and Training
42	
43	Custom decoder-only transformers with GPT-2-like architecture (pre-norm, GELU activation, causal mask). Size labels below are internal designations and do not correspond to OpenAI's GPT-2 checkpoints (e.g., our "large" at 86M is smaller than GPT-2 Small at 117M):
44	
45	| Config | Layers | d_model | Heads | Parameters |
46	|--------|--------|---------|-------|------------|
47	| tiny | 4 | 256 | 4 | ~3.5M |
48	| small | 6 | 384 | 6 | ~12M |
49	| medium | 8 | 512 | 8 | ~26M |
50	| large | 12 | 768 | 12 | ~86M |
51	
52	Optimizer: AdamW (weight_decay=0.01), cosine decay with linear warmup, lr=3e-4, seq_len=256, batch_size=32, 5000 steps. All experiments use 4 random seeds for core conditions (2 where noted). Learning curves confirm behavioral plateau by step 3000--4000 at all sizes (Appendix E). Denoising experiments use PyTorch; multi-rule and Wikipedia experiments use MLX. All problems are generated and verified by SymPy; no human annotation is involved. Full generation templates are provided in the supplementary material.
53	
54	### 3.3 Corpus Design
55	
56	**Denoising setup (primary).** A generator creates mathematical problems of four types: multi-step arithmetic, factoring, equation solving, and differentiation, formatted as step-by-step derivations in English. The key design: **each problem appears with contradictory answers** -- modeling the scenario where the same question receives conflicting responses. The tokenizer is character-level (vocab ~57); BPE robustness is verified in Section 4.4.
57	
58	| Condition | Correct | Incorrect | Ratio | Purpose |
59	|-----------|:-------:|:---------:|:-----:|---------|
60	| Equal random | 1 | 1 random | 1:1 | Signal extraction from random noise |
61	| Equal coherent | 1 | 1 coherent | 1:1 | Control: coherent errors eliminate bias |
62	| 2:1 noise | 1 | 2 random | 1:2 | Noise tolerance at moderate noise |
63	| 4:1 noise | 1 | 4 random | 1:4 | Noise tolerance at high noise |
64	
65	Each denoising corpus contains 5,000 unique problems. Standard (non-denoising) experiments where each problem appears once are reported in Appendix C.
66	
67	**Wikipedia setup (transfer).** To test generalization beyond formal math, we construct corpora from 20,000 Wikipedia paragraphs. Using spaCy NER, we create two corruption modes: *random substitution* (each entity replaced with a random entity of the same type) and *coherent substitution* (a consistent global mapping, e.g., every "France" -> "Japan", every "Paris" -> "Tokyo"). Coherent substitution preserves grammatical structure and cross-entity consistency within each paragraph; random substitution breaks co-occurrence patterns (e.g., a paragraph may mention "Kumamoto" in a context about French history). Both modes substitute entities of matching type, so surface-level grammatical anomalies (gender, case agreement) are minimal. Models train on 50/50 mixes; evaluation uses 2,000 held-out paired paragraphs.
68	
69	### 3.4 Evaluation
70	
71	**Paired evaluation (primary).** For each problem, a shared prompt is generated with two completions (correct and incorrect). NLL is computed on completion tokens only, conditioned on the prompt. The primary metric is **pair accuracy**: the fraction of pairs where the model assigns lower NLL to the correct completion. This is equivalent to the Common Language Effect Size (CLES; McGraw & Wong, 1992). Length-matched evaluation confirms that accuracy is not driven by completion length differences (within 1 pp across all conditions).
72	
73	**Generative evaluation.** Greedy decoding on 500 test prompts with automated SymPy verification. This confirms that the discriminative effect extends to generation (Section 4.5; Table 4).

92	*Figure 1. The central contrast. Random errors: accuracy scales from 65% to 85%. Coherent errors: accuracy stays near chance across all sizes.*
93	
94	When the same problem appears with both a correct and a random wrong answer, the model learns to prefer the correct one -- accuracy reaches 85% at 86M parameters. When the wrong answer follows a coherent rule system, the effect disappears: accuracy hovers near 50% at all scales. This is the paper's central result.
95	
96	Random accuracy increases monotonically with scale (65% -> 75% -> 81% -> 85%). Coherent accuracy converges toward 50% from below, consistent with the MDL prediction that equally compressible systems at equal frequency should be indistinguishable. The below-chance coherent accuracy at tiny (~43%) reflects textual simplicity of the false rules (e.g., dropping terms in derivatives); the compressor prefers whichever system is simpler to encode. The multi-rule experiment (Section 4.2) controls for this surface-form effect.
97	
98	### 4.2 Multi-Rule Errors: The Sharp Crossover
99	
100	The denoising experiments establish two poles: one coherent rule yields chance, random errors yield strong bias. We probe the boundary by training on corpora with N alternative wrong rules per task type, where each problem's rule is chosen at random. Each rule is compact, but the mapping "problem -> rule" is unpredictable.

105	|:-------:|:--------:|:-:|

107	| 2 | 77.6% +/- 1.3% | < 10^-6 |
108	| 3 | 82.8% +/- 0.1% | < 10^-6 |
109	| 5 | 84.8% +/- 0.5% | < 10^-6 |

118	This is the most mechanistically revealing result. With one false rule, $K(T_2) \approx K(T_1)$. With two rules, the learner must encode a random selector -- which rule applies to which problem -- adding high-complexity bits that break compressibility. At N=10, accuracy (88.3%) exceeds even the random baseline (79.5%), because multi-rule errors combine compactness of individual rules with incompressibility of the selector.

120	### 4.3 Transfer to Real Text: Wikipedia
121	
122	**Table 3.** Wikipedia entity substitution (50/50, 4 seeds per size).
123	
124	| Size | Random Accuracy | Coherent Accuracy |
125	|------|:--------------:|:-----------------:|
126	| tiny (3.5M) | **69.6% +/- 0.1%** | 48.7% +/- 0.5% |

131	The random/coherent contrast reproduces: random substitution yields 70--71% accuracy (all p < 10^-100), coherent substitution yields 46--49% (at or below chance). The effect is weaker than in math (71% vs 85%) because natural language provides more textual flexibility for locally fluent errors. Unlike math, Wikipedia accuracy saturates near 70% across the tested range -- the compressibility gap in natural language does not substantially benefit from additional capacity at this scale. Per-entity-type accuracy ranges from 82% (LOC) to 61% (CARDINAL), with geographic entities showing the strongest effect (Appendix D).
132	
133	### 4.4 Robustness Checks
134	
135	**Tokenization.** The primary experiments use a character-level tokenizer (vocab ~57). To verify that the effect is not an artifact of tokenization, we repeat the core conditions with a BPE tokenizer (SentencePiece, vocab=1000). The effect survives and strengthens: random accuracy increases from 65.3% to 75.9% in denoising (79.5% to 85.6% in standard); coherent accuracy remains at chance (49.3% and 45.9% respectively). Full results in Appendix E.
136	
137	**Standard (non-denoising) setup.** In the standard setup, each problem appears once with either a correct or incorrect solution (no within-problem contradiction). Paired accuracy is higher (79.5--88.3% vs 65.3--85.2%), but the gap closes with scale (from 14 pp at tiny to 3 pp at large). Critically, the random/coherent contrast holds identically: standard coherent accuracy is 47--52%, at chance. Full comparison in Appendix C.
138	
139	**Frequency vs compressibility.** For random errors, truth bias persists even when correct examples are a small minority: 67% paired accuracy at 10/90 correct-to-incorrect ratio (Appendix C). For coherent errors, the pattern reverses -- frequency alone determines preference: at 40/60 coherent, the model follows the majority with 72% preference for the false system; at 20/80, this rises to 91%. This asymmetry is a direct prediction of the MDL framework: when both systems compress equally well, frequency is the only tiebreaker.
140	
141	**Matched-control ablation.** A potential confound is that coherent errors affect more derivation steps per problem (~3 on average) than standard random errors (exactly 1). To control for this, we generate "matched random" errors that corrupt the same number of steps as coherent errors but at random positions with random values. Matched-random accuracy is 74.5% +/- 0.4% (4 seeds) -- lower than standard random (79.5%, which corrupts only 1 step) but far above coherent (47.2%, which also corrupts ~3 steps). The 27 pp gap between matched-random and coherent at identical step counts confirms that compressibility, not surface-level corruption load, drives the random/coherent contrast.
142	
143	**Noise tolerance.** The correct signal persists under increasing noise ratios (1:2 and 1:4 random), with accuracy degrading gracefully from 85% to 66--75% at large scale. Details in Appendix C.
144	
145	### 4.5 Generative Evaluation
146	
147	Paired evaluation is a forced-choice (discriminative) setting. To verify that the effect extends to generation, we run greedy decoding with SymPy verification on 500 problems per model across all sizes.
148	
149	**Table 4.** Generative accuracy (4 seeds, 500 problems each).
150	
151	| Size | Random-trained | Coherent-trained | Gap |
152	|------|:--------------:|:----------------:|:---:|
153	| tiny (3.5M) | 30.5% +/- 1.7% | 20.8% +/- 3.6% | +9.7 pp |
154	| small (12M) | 46.7% +/- 1.9% | 31.7% +/- 1.8% | +15.0 pp |
155	| medium (26M) | 50.5% +/- 1.3% | 36.1% +/- 2.7% | +14.4 pp |
156	| large (86M) | 52.8% +/- 1.1% | 35.6% +/- 1.8% | +17.2 pp |
157	
158	The gap widens with model size (10 pp at tiny -> 17 pp at large), confirming that the discriminative preference translates into a generative advantage. The absolute generation accuracy is substantially lower than paired accuracy (53% vs 85% at large). This gap reflects the fundamental difference between discrimination and generation: the model may assign higher likelihood to correct completions while lacking the capacity to produce them reliably from scratch. Both metrics agree on the direction: random-trained models outperform coherent-trained ones at all scales.
159	
160	Per-task breakdown reveals that generation difficulty varies sharply by type. For random-trained large models: derivative (79.5%), algebra (70.4%), equation (61.0%), arithmetic (0%). Multi-step arithmetic requires exact carry propagation across many digits -- a well-known difficulty for autoregressive generation at this scale -- even though paired evaluation shows strong discrimination. Coherent-trained models show lower generation accuracy across all types, with the gap largest for algebra (70.4% vs 38.3%) and smallest for derivative (79.5% vs 56.5%).

181	The experiments above use synthetic math-only corpora. To test whether the effect survives in a more realistic setting, we train a Qwen3-1B model (~1B parameters) from scratch on a mixed corpus: 1 GB of FineWeb-Edu (Penedo et al., 2024) with mathematical content filtered out, plus our contradictory math corpus repeated to constitute ~8% of the total. 192	This result addresses two key concerns. First, the effect is not confined to small models: it persists at 1B parameters, the largest scale tested. The compression-consistency contrast survives even when the model has substantial capacity devoted to other tasks.

195	
196	## 5. Discussion
197	
198	**Summary of findings.** The experiments support a unified hypothesis: **in our controlled settings, the compression objective tracks consistency rather than truth.** Any internally consistent rule system -- true or false -- compresses equally well. Truth bias emerges only when false alternatives are structurally incoherent. The evidence converges across setups (denoising, standard, Wikipedia), error types (random, coherent, multi-rule), and evaluation modes (discriminative and generative).
199	
200	**The role of frequency.** Our results reveal an asymmetry between random and coherent error regimes. For random errors, compressibility overrides frequency: truth bias persists even at 10/90 correct-to-incorrect ratio (67% accuracy). For coherent errors, frequency is decisive: at 40/60 coherent mixing, the model follows the majority with 72% preference for the false system, rising to 91% at 20/80. This is a direct MDL prediction -- when competing systems compress equally well, the more frequent one wins. The practical implication is that coherent falsehood needs no frequency advantage to neutralize truth bias; equal frequency suffices.
201	
202	**What the multi-rule experiment shows.** The sharp N=1->2 transition is the strongest evidence that compressibility, not surface-form complexity, is the operative variable. Each individual rule at N=2 remains compact, yet the random assignment of rules to problems introduces incompressible bits. This rules out explanations based on lexical diversity or number of corrupted tokens alone.
203	
204	**Implications and scope.** In our controlled settings, the training objective does not provide an inherent "truth compass." Internally coherent falsehood remains competitive. Embedding cross-domain verification can partially restore truth bias, but the effect exhibits inverse scaling: verification accuracy drops from 71% at 3.5M to 61% at 86M parameters (Appendix E), as larger models absorb coherent within-domain patterns more readily. This has potential relevance for alignment (systematic false beliefs may resist filtering and grow harder to verify at scale), hallucinations (coherent misconceptions may persist; cf. Kalai & Vempala, 2024), and data curation (diverse errors are filtered effectively, but coordinated errors may not be). However, all our experiments use models up to 86M parameters trained from scratch on synthetic or semi-synthetic data. Whether these findings extend to large-scale pretraining on heterogeneous real-world corpora remains an open question. We view our results as identifying a mechanism and its boundary conditions, not as direct claims about production LLMs.
205	
206	Our findings admit an interpretive analogy with Popper's falsifiability criterion (1959): a false theory that requires ad hoc corrections to accommodate observations has higher description length than a true one. The analogy is limited -- the model does not "test" theories -- but the structural parallel is suggestive.
207	
208	---
209	
210	## 6. Conclusion
211	
212	In controlled experiments with transformers from 3.5M to 1B parameters, the compression objective tracks consistency rather than truth. Models prefer the correct answer only when errors are structurally incoherent; a single coherent alternative rule eliminates this preference, and two competing rules restore it. The same pattern reproduces on real Wikipedia text, in generative evaluation, across architectures (GPT-2 and Qwen3), and at 1B scale on a mixed real-text corpus where math is a minority of the training data. In the settings we study, correctness preference is a compression artifact whose presence or absence is determined by the description length of the false answer system. Whether this mechanism extends to full-scale pretraining on heterogeneous real-world corpora remains the key open question.
213	
214	## Limitations
215	
216	**Model scale.** Core experiments use models from 3.5M to 86M parameters, with robustness checks at 420M (Qwen3-0.6B) and ~1B (Qwen3-1B on FineWeb-Edu + math). The random/coherent contrast persists across this range, but we have not tested beyond 1B. For coherent errors, an MDL argument suggests the result should hold regardless of scale: equally compressible systems at equal frequency offer no basis for preference.
217	
218	**Domain specificity.** Mathematics provides an unusually crisp correct/incorrect distinction. The effect weakens in natural language (71% vs 85%), where errors can remain locally fluent. Extending to domains with competing real-world knowledge systems (e.g., conflicting news sources, historical revisionism) remains open.
219	
220	**Discriminative vs generative gap.** Paired accuracy (85%) substantially exceeds generative accuracy (53%) at large scale. This gap matters: real-world LLM behavior depends on generation, not discrimination. Several factors may contribute: generation requires producing correct tokens autoregressively (errors compound), while paired evaluation only requires relative likelihood ranking. The gap narrows with model size (49 pp at tiny, 32 pp at large), suggesting that scale partially bridges it. Critically, both metrics agree on the direction and on the random/coherent contrast at all tested scales -- the discriminative preference is not an evaluation artifact. Still, paired accuracy should be interpreted as a controlled diagnostic of model preference, not as a direct measure of output truthfulness.
221	
222	**Seed counts.** Core conditions use 4 seeds; some conditions use 2. Sufficient for directional stability, not for tight confidence intervals.
223	
224	**Causal identification.** While we vary error structure as the independent variable, random and coherent corruptions may differ in additional ways (number of affected derivation steps, surface-form simplicity). The multi-rule experiment partially addresses this by holding individual rule complexity constant, but a fully matched control (identical corruption load, lexical diversity, and output length) would strengthen the causal claim.
225	
226	**Future directions.** Linear probing for "truth directions" vs "coherence directions" in models trained under our conditions could connect behavioral findings to the internal representation literature. Testing interactions with RLHF and investigating whether the compressibility mechanism interacts with in-context learning are natural extensions.
227	
228	## Ethical Considerations
229	
230	This work demonstrates that, in controlled settings, internally consistent misinformation may be harder for language models to filter than diverse errors. The finding does not directly demonstrate vulnerability in production models. We believe transparent reporting of conditions under which truth bias fails is more beneficial than concealment, as it can inform defensive measures in data curation and model evaluation.

323	## Appendix B: Formal MDL Statement
324	
325	We state the theoretical prediction that motivates our experiments more precisely.
326	
327	**Proposition (informal MDL prediction).** Consider a corpus $D$ containing $n$ problems, each appearing with two answers: one generated by theory $T_1$ (correct) and one by theory $T_2$ (alternative). An ideal MDL learner selects the theory $T^*$ minimizing total description length $L(T) + L(D|T)$.
328	
329	- *Case 1 (random errors):* $T_1$ is a compact rule system with $L(T_1) = O(1)$. $T_2$ assigns independent random answers, requiring $L(T_2|D) = O(n)$. The MDL learner prefers $T_1$ for sufficiently large $n$.
330	- *Case 2 (coherent errors):* Both $T_1$ and $T_2$ are compact rule systems with $L(T_1) \approx L(T_2) = O(1)$. At equal frequency, $L(D|T_1) \approx L(D|T_2)$, and the MDL learner has no basis to prefer one over the other.
331	- *Case 3 (multi-rule, $N$ competing false rules):* Each rule $r_i$ is compact ($L(r_i) = O(1)$), but encoding the assignment of rules to problems requires an additional selector $s: \{1,...,n\} \to \{1,...,N\}$ with $L(s) = O(n \log N)$ when assignments are random. Total false-system description length grows as $O(n \log N)$, restoring the MDL advantage of $T_1$.
332	
333	This proposition is not a formal theorem about neural network training dynamics — it describes the behavior of an idealized MDL learner. Our experiments test whether gradient-descent-trained transformers approximate this behavior. The results in Sections 4.1--4.3 are consistent with all three predictions.

Я изучил основные документы проекта. Вот краткое резюме вашей научной работы:

---

## "Error Structure Determines Correctness Preference in Contradictory Training Data"

### Главная гипотеза
Сжатие (next-token prediction как минимизация длины описания) предпочитает **согласованность**, а не **истину**. Truth bias — это побочный эффект несжимаемости случайных ошибок.

### Ключевые результаты

1. **Случайные ошибки** → модель предпочитает правильные ответы (65%→85% с ростом размера модели)
2. **Когерентные ошибки** (одно альтернативное правило) → предпочтение исчезает (~45-51%, на уровне шанса)
3. **Мульти-правило** (N≥2) → резкий фазовый переход: N=1→47%, N=2→78% — две конкурирующие ложные теории достаточно для восстановления truth bias
4. **Wikipedia** → эффект воспроизводится на реальном тексте (71% vs 46%), но слабее чем в математике
5. **Масштабирование до 1B** (Qwen3 на FineWeb-Edu + 8% мат.) → 77% vs 47%, контраст сохраняется
6. **Частотная асимметрия** — для случайных ошибок truth bias держится даже при 10/90; для когерентных — чистая частота определяет предпочтение

### Статус
- **Опубликовано на arXiv**: arxiv.org/abs/2603.11749
- **170+ обученных моделей**, модели от 3.5M до 1B параметров
- **Целевая конференция**: NeurIPS 2026 (дедлайн абстракта — 4 мая)
- Есть рецензия Алёны Феногеновой (`Статья К.К..md`) с конкретными рекомендациями по доработке структуры, абстракта, интро, методологии

### Основные замечания рецензента (Феногенова)
- Абстракт перегружен деталями — нужна структура "проблема → гипотеза → метод → вклад"
- Интро не обосновывает актуальность проблемы
- Вклад один и чёткий: **экспериментально доказанная гипотеза** (формат short paper)
- Генеративная оценка нужна на всех размерах (сделано — Table 4)
- Нужен overview/research hypothesis в начале методологии
- J5 и BPE — рискованные аблейшены для short paper

Что бы вы хотели сделать дальше?

Что думаешь про этот отзыв ИИ ревьювера? С чем согласен? ultrathink Да — формально на NeurIPS 2026 эту работу подать можно. Для main track в этом году дедлайны такие: abstract — 4 мая 2026 AOE, полный paper с supplementary — 6 мая 2026 AOE. По типу вклада работа тоже подходит: call for papers прямо приветствует in-depth analysis of existing methods, если она дает новые инсайты об их поведении и ограничениях. Публичные arXiv/GitHub сами по себе не запрещены, но анонимная submission, supplement и любые внешние ссылки/код для ревью должны оставаться анонимными; прямую ссылку на текущий публичный репозиторий в анонимной PDF я бы не ставил.

По качеству я бы сейчас оценил это как скорее 3/6 = borderline reject, а не accept. Не потому, что идея слабая — идея как раз хорошая, — а потому что у NeurIPS main track “3” как раз соответствует technically solid paper, где ограниченность evaluation перевешивает плюсы. У вас сильный центральный результат, но доказательная база пока слишком узкая для уверенного main-track acceptance.

Что в работе реально сильное: очень чистая controlled setup; запоминающийся и нетривиальный главный контраст между random и coherent errors; резкий переход в multi-rule experiment; перенос на Wikipedia; проверка на другой архитектуре; и дополнительный 1B mixed-corpus experiment. В таком виде paper читается как hypothesis-driven empirical study, а не как просто очередной benchmark. Это хороший фундамент для сильной конференционной работы.

Что мешает пройти сейчас: ядро статьи все еще опирается в основном на модели 3.5M–86M, у largest GPT-style условий только 2 seeds, у ~1B mixed-corpus эксперимента вообще 1 seed, а gap между paired и generative evaluation на large очень большой — paper сам подчеркивает примерно 85% vs 53%. Плюс сами авторы честно пишут, что внешний validity ограничен: математика дает слишком “чистую” границу true/false, эффект в natural language слабее, а causal identification пока не идеально matched. Это как раз тот набор замечаний, из-за которого на NeurIPS paper легко получает “интересно, но evaluation narrow”.

Что я бы обязательно доделал до подачи:

Добавить более реалистичные knowledge-conflict evaluation. Сейчас related work уже ссылается на Li et al. про conflicting knowledge, но в статье не видно более прямой линии к литературе по knowledge conflicts: Resolving Knowledge Conflicts in Large Language Models, DYNAMICQA, и EMNLP 2024 survey по knowledge conflicts. Их стоит и процитировать, и по возможности использовать как внешний validation target.
Усилить статистику и контроли. Нужны больше seeds на верхних масштабах, confidence intervals везде, и еще более жесткий matched control по corruption load / lexical diversity / output length. Paper уже сам признает, что some conditions use 2 seeds and causal matching is not complete — это надо закрывать до ревью.
Сделать MDL-аргумент строже. Сейчас формальная часть честно названа informal MDL prediction, то есть это пока не теорема про training dynamics, а идеализированная мотивация; gzip-appendix тоже выглядит как supporting evidence, а не как жесткое доказательство механизма. Для NeurIPS это место почти наверняка атакуют. Я бы либо усилил теорию, либо сильнее смягчил claim и сделал акцент на empirical regularity.
Починить framing и naming. README-слоган “language models don’t learn truth...” звучит заметно шире, чем то, что paper реально показывает в controlled settings. Плюс обозначения Qwen3-0.6B и особенно Qwen3-1B сейчас вводят в заблуждение: официальный Qwen3 dense lineup — это 0.6B, 1.7B, 4B, 8B, 14B, 32B; официального “Qwen3-1B” нет. Если это ваши кастомные Qwen3-style модели, так и надо написать. Иначе reviewer может посчитать это фактической ошибкой.

По фактам и библиографии: “дымящегося пистолета” в виде полностью вымышленных ссылок я не нашел. Я отдельно проверил самые подозрительные recent entries, и ключевые работы действительно существуют: Li et al. 2024 про conflicting knowledge, Joshi et al. 2024 про personas and truthfulness, Bürger et al. 2024, Kalai & Vempala 2024, Wan & Mei 2025, Pan et al. 2025, Ravfogel et al. 2025, Chlon et al. 2025. То есть список литературы выглядит не как чистая галлюцинация, а как реальный, но плохо дочищенный список.

Но следы полуавтоматической сборки / LLM-assisted editing в библиографии есть. Самые явные: в референсе Wan & Mei остался мусорный хвост "[Authors: Jun Wan, Lingrui Mei]"; в тексте стоит “Burger et al.”, хотя в реальной публикации фамилия Bürger; в тексте cited “Marks & Tegmark, 2023”, а в references — 2024, что нужно унифицировать; запись Chlon et al. у вас использует старое название Why Language Models Actually Hallucinate, тогда как текущая arXiv-версия уже называется Order Sensitivity and Information Budgeting...; и ссылка на Huang et al. слегка расплывчата — paper пишет примерно r ~ -0.95, хотя в самой работе общий Pearson reported как -0.93, а значения около -0.95 относятся к отдельным подзадачам. Это не доказывает “сгенерированность” статьи, но очень похоже на bibliography, собранную полуавтоматически и не вычитанную вручную.

Есть еще и versioning-traces. arXiv сейчас идет под заголовком Truth as a Compression Artifact in Language Model Training, а текущий GitHub/README — под Error Structure Determines Correctness Preference...; README говорит про 210+ models (3.5M–420M), appendix в paper — про over 170 models, а текущий abstract paper уже говорит о 3.5M–1B. Это не криминал, но для NeurIPS лучше иметь одну чистую и консистентную историю, а не три слегка расходящиеся версии.

Мой отзыв в форме ревью был бы примерно таким: “Interesting, clear, and potentially important controlled study. The central empirical contrast is memorable and the paper presents a plausible compression-based account of why correctness preference emerges under random but not coherent false supervision. However, the current evidence is still too narrow for NeurIPS main track: core results are mostly synthetic/semi-synthetic, high-scale evidence is thin, the MDL account remains suggestive rather than established, and the framing occasionally overreaches the demonstrated scope. I recommend rejection in the current form, but I would encourage a stronger resubmission after broader real-world conflict evaluation, tighter statistics, and a full bibliography/metadata cleanup.”

Итог: подавать можно, но я бы не подавал “как есть”. Мой диагноз — хорошая работа с реальным потенциалом, но пока еще не полностью выведенная в NeurIPS-ready состояние. Самая важная задача не “придумать еще одну красивую историю”, а сузить claim, расширить real-world validation и вылизать библиографию/метаданные.

content.text:
Хороший отзыв, в целом адекватный. Разберу по пунктам — с чем согласен, с чем нет.

---

## Согласен полностью

**Библиография/метаданные — нужна чистка.** Хвост `[Authors: Jun Wan, Lingrui Mei]`, Burger vs Bürger, Marks 2023 vs 2024, устаревшее название Chlon et al. — это всё реальные проблемы, которые ревьюер NeurIPS заметит и которые создают впечатление небрежности. Чинится за час, но важно.

**Versioning traces.** Разные заголовки на arXiv, GitHub и в paper, разное число моделей (170 vs 210+) — нужна консистентность. Согласен.

**Qwen3-1B naming.** Ревьюер прав — официального Qwen3-1B нет в линейке. Нужно явно написать "custom Qwen3-architecture model with ~1B parameters", иначе это выглядит как фактическая ошибка.

**Seeds на больших масштабах.** 2 seeds на large GPT-2, 1 seed на 1B — это действительно слабое место. Ревьюер NeurIPS почти наверняка это отметит.

---

## Согласен частично

**"Evaluation narrow" / synthetic-only.** Замечание справедливо, но ревьюер недооценивает то, что уже есть: Wikipedia experiment — это реальный текст, а 1B на FineWeb-Edu — это реалистичный setup с 92% настоящего текста. Проблема не в том, что нет real-world данных, а в том, что paper недостаточно это акцентирует. Wikipedia + FineWeb — это уже два шага за пределы чистой синтетики.

**MDL-аргумент.** Ревьюер прав, что gzip appendix — supporting evidence, а не proof. Но предложение "усилить теорию" расплывчато — формальная теорема о training dynamics трансформеров при MDL это отдельная работа на год. Правильный путь — тот, который paper уже частично выбрал: позиционировать MDL как мотивирующий framework, а не как доказанный механизм. Нужно лишь убедиться, что нигде в тексте claim не перешагивает за "consistent with MDL prediction".

**Knowledge conflicts литература.** Ревьюер прав, что ссылки на knowledge conflicts survey стоит добавить. Но предложение "использовать как external validation target" — сомнительно. Существующие knowledge conflict бенчмарки (DYNAMICQA и пр.) работают с pretrained моделями и RAG, а не с training from scratch. Это ортогональный setup. Процитировать — да, строить на них validation — нет.

---

## Не согласен

**Оценка 3/6 = borderline reject.** Считаю заниженной. Ревьюер сам перечисляет сильные стороны: чистый setup, запоминающийся контраст, multi-rule фазовый переход, Wikipedia transfer, две архитектуры, 1B scale. Это не borderline reject, это 5/10 с потенциалом на weak accept при чистке. Основная слабость (seeds, bibliography) — техническая, не концептуальная. Для NeurIPS главное — novelty и insight, а здесь они есть.

**"Gap между paired и generative — проблема."** Не согласен, что это слабость. Paper честно обсуждает этот gap, показывает что он *сужается* с масштабом (49pp→32pp), и оба метрики согласуются по направлению. Это не баг, это feature — различие discrimination vs generation хорошо известно. Ревьюер скорее ищет к чему прицепиться.

**"Framing overreaches."** Ревьюер указывает на README-слоган, но README — не paper. В самой v3 claims уже аккуратно ограничены: "in controlled settings", "in the settings we study", "remains an open question". Это уже сделано.

**Matched control "не идеально".** В v3 уже есть matched-random ablation (Section 4.4): те же ~3 испорченных шага, случайные значения → 74.5% vs когерентные 47.2%. 27pp gap при одинаковой corruption load. Ревьюер либо не заметил, либо хочет ещё более строгий контроль по lexical diversity — но это уже diminishing returns.

---

## Что реально нужно сделать до подачи

1. **Больше seeds** на large (4 вместо 2) и хотя бы 2-3 на 1B — самая критичная доработка
2. **Чистка библиографии** — механическая работа
3. **Уточнить Qwen3 naming** — одна правка
4. **Синхронизировать метаданные** (число моделей, заголовок) между paper/arXiv/GitHub
5. **Добавить 2-3 ссылки** на knowledge conflicts литературу в Related Work

2	
3	### Language models don't learn truth. They learn whatever compresses best.
4	
5	When a model trains on contradictory data — the same question with both correct and incorrect answers — which answer does it prefer? We trained 210+ transformers (3.5M–420M params) to find out.
6	
7	**The answer: truth wins only when lies are messy.**
8	
9	[![arXiv](https://img.shields.io/badge/arXiv-2603.11749-b31b1b.svg)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11749)
10	[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-green.svg)](LICENSE)
11	
12	---
13	
14	## The Experiment
15	
16	We train GPT-2 style models (3.5M–86M params) on math problems where each problem appears with **both correct and incorrect solutions**. Then we ask: which solution does the model prefer?
17	
18	| Error type | What happens | Accuracy |
19	|------------|-------------|:--------:|
20	| **Random errors** (each wrong answer is unique) | Model finds the truth | 65% → 85% with scale |
21	| **Coherent errors** (one systematic wrong rule) | Model can't tell the difference | ~50% (chance) |
22	| **Two competing wrong rules** | Truth bias snaps back | 78% |
23	
24	One coherent lie kills truth bias. Two competing lies restore it. This is an **abrupt crossover**, not a gradual effect.
25	
26	## The Principle
27	
28	We call this the **Compression–Consistency Principle**:
29	
30	> Gradient descent favors the most compressible answer cluster, not truth per se.
31	
32	Random errors are all different — they can't compress into a rule. The correct answer is the only pattern, so the model learns it. But a coherent false system (e.g., "always subtract 1 before multiplying") is just as compact as the true rules. The compressor sees no difference.
33	
34	**Why does adding a second wrong rule break this?** Because now the model needs a *selector* — which rule applies to which problem. This selector is a random function with high Kolmogorov complexity. Even though each individual rule is compact, the selector can't compress, and that's what restores truth bias.
35	
36	**Truth bias is not a feature of intelligence. It's a compression artifact.**
37	
38	## Key Results
39	
40	**Denoising (core experiment):**
41	- Random errors: accuracy scales 65% → 75% → 81% → 85% (3.5M → 86M)
42	- Coherent errors: stuck at ~45–51% across all sizes
43	- More noise degrades gracefully: 1:2 ratio → 75%, 1:4 ratio → 66%
44	
45	**Multi-rule phase transition:**
46	- 1 wrong rule → 47% (chance). 2 wrong rules → 78% (tiny) / 86% (small). 10 rules → 88%
47	- The jump from 1→2 rules is the critical moment where lies stop compressing
48	
49	**Real text (Wikipedia):**
50	- Replace entities randomly ("Paris" → "Kumamoto"): model detects it, 71% accuracy
51	- Replace consistently ("France"→"Japan", "Paris"→"Tokyo"): model can't tell, 46%
52	
53	**Compression gap correlates with accuracy:**
54	- gzip compression ratio gap between correct/incorrect completions correlates with model behavior across 9 conditions (Spearman ρ = 0.68, p = 0.042)
55	
56	## Why This Matters
57	
58	**For alignment:** The training objective alone is not a "truth compass." A well-crafted consistent lie compresses just as well as truth. Scale won't fix this — coherent errors stay at chance from 3.5M to 86M.
59	
60	**For understanding hallucinations:** Coherent misconceptions can persist because they compress well — not because the model lacks capacity. Internally consistent hallucinations may be the hardest to eliminate.
61	
62	**For disinformation:** Coordinated campaigns that maintain internal consistency — always the same false narrative, always the same fabricated sources — function as a compressible alternative rule system. Our results predict that such coherent falsehood is indistinguishable from truth for the compressor, unlike diverse organic errors that fragment into incompressible noise.
63	
64	**For data curation:** At 4:1 noise ratio, models still extract truth at 65% accuracy — as long as errors are structurally incoherent. Diverse mistakes are self-correcting; systematic ones are not.
65	
66	## Quick Start
67	
68	```bash
69	# Setup
70	python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
71	pip install -r requirements.txt
72	
73	# Generate corpus: same problems with correct + random wrong answers
74	python data/generate_math.py \
75	  --n 200000 --ratio 0.5 --seed 42 --error-mode random \
76	  --output data/corpus/train_mixed_50_50.txt
77	
78	# Train a model
79	python training/train.py \
80	  --corpus data/corpus/train_mixed_50_50.txt \
81	  --model tiny --steps 5000 --seed 42 \
82	  --output results/mixed_50_50_tiny_seed42
83	
84	# Evaluate: does it prefer the correct answer?
85	python data/generate_paired_test.py \
86	  --n 5000 --seed 999 --error-mode random \
87	  --output data/corpus/test_paired_random.jsonl
88	
89	python training/eval_paired.py \
90	  --model-size tiny \
91	  --weights results/mixed_50_50_tiny_seed42/model_final.npz \
92	  --tokenizer results/mixed_50_50_tiny_seed42/tokenizer.json \
93	  --test-paired data/corpus/test_paired_random.jsonl \
94	  --output results/mixed_50_50_tiny_seed42/eval_paired.json
95	```

content.content: 5	Language models trained on contradictory data often prefer correct answers, yet the mechanism behind this preference is poorly understood. Without such understanding, we cannot predict when this implicit filtering will fail -- a question critical for controlling model behavior on noisy real-world corpora. We hypothesize that next-token prediction, as a compression process, favors whichever answer cluster has lower description length; truth benefits only when errors lack internal structure. We train transformers (3.5M--1B parameters) from scratch on controlled corpora where the same problem appears with both correct and incorrect solutions, systematically varying the structure of errors. We demonstrate three findings: (a) when errors are random, models develop a correctness preference scaling from 65% to 85% with model size; (b) when errors follow a single coherent alternative rule, this preference vanishes entirely (~45--51%); (c) the transition is sharp -- two competing wrong rules suffice to restore correctness preference (47% -> 78%). The pattern reproduces on real Wikipedia text (71% vs 46%), across architectures (GPT-2 and Qwen3), and at 1B scale on a mixed real-text corpus (77% vs 47%). These results support the hypothesis that, in controlled contradictory corpora, model preference is driven by the relative compressibility of competing answer systems rather than by truth per se.

328	%======================================================================
329	
330	\textbf{Model scale.}\quad The primary experiments use models of 3.5M--86M parameters. Section~\ref{sec:qwen1b} extends to Qwen3-1B (${\sim}$1B parameters) trained on real text, confirming the random/coherent contrast at larger scale. However, this remains far below the scale of production LLMs (7B--400B+), and the 1B result uses only a single seed per condition. For coherent errors, an MDL argument suggests the result should hold regardless of scale: equally compressible systems at equal frequency offer no basis for preference. Replication at multi-billion scale with compute-matched training remains an important next step.
331	
332	\textbf{Domain specificity.}\quad Mathematics provides an unusually crisp correct/incorrect distinction. The effect weakens in natural language (71\% vs 85\%), where errors can remain locally fluent. Extending to domains with competing real-world knowledge systems (e.g., conflicting news sources, historical revisionism) remains open.

Continue with other work or respond to the user instead. - **Foreground vs background**: Use foreground (default) when you need the agent's results before you can proceed — e.g., research agents whose findings inform your next steps. Use background when you have genuinely independent work to do in parallel. - To continue a previously spawned agent, use SendMessage with the agent's ID or name as the `to` field. The working directory persists between commands, but shell state does not. The shell environment is initialized from the user's profile (bash or zsh).

# Instructions

 - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes in your command (e.g., cd "path with spaces/file.txt")

You do not need to check the output right away - you'll be notified when it finishes. You do not need to use '&' at the end of the command when using this parameter.

  - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together.

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. The numbered steps below indicate which commands should be batched in parallel.

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive git commands (push --force, reset --hard, checkout ., restore ., clean -f, branch -D) unless the user explicitly requests these actions. Taking unauthorized destructive actions is unhelpful and can result in lost work, so it's best to ONLY run these commands when given direct instructions 
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- CRITICAL: Always create NEW commits rather than amending, unless the user explicitly requests a git amend. When a pre-commit hook fails, the commit did NOT happen — so --amend would modify the PREVIOUS commit, which may result in destroying work or losing previous changes. Instead, after hook failure, fix the issue, re-stage, and create a NEW commit
- When staging files, prefer adding specific files by name rather than using "git add -A" or "git add .", which can accidentally include sensitive files (.env, credentials) or large binaries
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive

1. Run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files. IMPORTANT: Never use the -uall flag as it can cause memory issues on large repos.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. Run the following commands in parallel:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message ending with:
   Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook: fix the issue and create a NEW commit

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Agent tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- IMPORTANT: Do not use --no-edit with git rebase commands, as the --no-edit flag is not a valid option for git rebase.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit
- In order to ensure good formatting, ALWAYS pass the commit message via a HEREDOC, a la this example:
<example>
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'

   Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
   EOF
   )"
</example>

# Creating pull requests
Use the gh command via the Bash tool for ALL GitHub-related tasks including working with issues, pull requests, checks, and releases. If given a Github URL use the gh command to get the information needed.

IMPORTANT: When the user asks you to create a pull request, follow these steps carefully:

   - Run a git log command and `git diff [base-branch]...HEAD` to understand the full commit history for the current branch (from the time it diverged from the base branch)
2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request title and summary:

   - Use the description/body for details, not the title
3. Run the following commands in parallel:

   - Push to remote with -u flag if needed
   - Create PR using gh pr create with the format below. Use a HEREDOC to pass the body to ensure correct formatting.

- Fast file pattern matching tool that works with any codebase size
- Supports glob patterns like "**/*.js" or "src/**/*.ts"

tools.name: Grep
tools.description:
A powerful search tool built on ripgrep

  Usage:
  - ALWAYS use Grep for search tasks. NEVER invoke `grep` or `rg` as a Bash command. The Grep tool has been optimized for correct permissions and access.
  - Supports full regex syntax (e.g., "log.*Error", "function\s+\w+")

  - Output modes: "content" shows matching lines, "files_with_matches" shows only file paths (default), "count" shows match counts
  - Use Agent tool for open-ended searches requiring multiple rounds
  - Pattern syntax: Uses ripgrep (not grep) - literal braces need escaping (use `interface\{\}` to find `interface{}` in Go code)

pattern.description: The regular expression pattern to search for in file contents

path.description: File or directory to search in (rg PATH). Defaults to current working directory.

glob.description: Glob pattern to filter files (e.g. "*.js", "*.{ts,tsx}") - maps to rg --glob

output_mode.description: Output mode: "content" shows matching lines (supports -A/-B/-C context, -n line numbers, head_limit), "files_with_matches" shows file paths (supports head_limit), "count" shows match counts (supports head_limit). Defaults to "files_with_matches".

-B.description: Number of lines to show before each match (rg -B). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise.

-A.description: Number of lines to show after each match (rg -A). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise.

context.description: Number of lines to show before and after each match (rg -C). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise.

-n.description: Show line numbers in output (rg -n). Requires output_mode: "content", ignored otherwise. Defaults to true.

type.description: File type to search (rg --type). Common types: js, py, rust, go, java, etc. More efficient than include for standard file types.

head_limit.description: Limit output to first N lines/entries, equivalent to "| head -N". Works across all output modes: content (limits output lines), files_with_matches (limits file paths), count (limits count entries). Defaults to 250 when unspecified. Pass 0 for unlimited (use sparingly — large result sets waste context).

Fetches full schema definitions for deferred tools so they can be called.

Deferred tools appear by name in <available-deferred-tools> messages. Until fetched, only the name is known — there is no parameter schema, so the tool cannot be invoked. This tool takes a query, matches it against the deferred tool list, and returns the matched tools' complete JSONSchema definitions inside a <functions> block. Once a tool's schema appears in that result, it is callable exactly like any tool defined at the top of the prompt.

Result format: each matched tool appears as one <function>{"description": "...", "name": "...", "parameters": {...}}</function> line inside the <functions> block — the same encoding as the tool list at the top of this prompt.

Query forms:
- "select:Read,Edit,Grep" — fetch these exact tools by name
- "notebook jupyter" — keyword search, up to max_results best matches
- "+slack send" — require "slack" in the name, rank by remaining terms

query.description: Query to find deferred tools. Use "select:<tool_name>" for direct selection, or keywords to search.

max_results.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 5)

tools.name: Write
tools.description:
Writes a file to the local filesystem.

Usage:
- This tool will overwrite the existing file if there is one at the provided path.
- If this is an existing file, you MUST use the Read tool first to read the file's contents. This tool will fail if you did not read the file first.
- Prefer the Edit tool for modifying existing files — it only sends the diff. Only use this tool to create new files or for complete rewrites.
- NEVER create documentation files (*.md) or README files unless explicitly requested by the User.
- Only use emojis if the user explicitly requests it. Avoid writing emojis to files unless asked.

file_path.description: The absolute path to the file to write (must be absolute, not relative)

content.description: The content to write to the file
